title: Classifier Deployment Checklist
concept: Verify latency, memory footprint, and handle out-of-vocabulary terms before deploying classification models to production. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: NER with Transformers.'

exercises.prompt: Why do we need a deployment checklist for our classification models?

options.text: To make sure the model is fast, safe, and doesn't crash on new words.
To make the model's accuracy reach exactly 100%.
To convert all text inputs into image files.

exercises.explain: Before deploying, we must make sure the model is fast, doesn't use too much memory, and can handle unexpected inputs without crashing.

cards.title: Ready for the Real World?
cards.body: You've built an amazing text classifier! But will it crash when thousands of users send millions of weird, misspelled messages all at once? Let's learn how to prep your model for the wild, chaotic real world.

cards.title: What is a Deployment Checklist?
cards.body: A deployment checklist is a set of engineering checks done before putting a classifier to work in the real world. We verify latency (how fast the model predicts), memory footprint (how much RAM it consumes), and out-of-vocabulary (OOV) handling (how it manages words it didn't see during training).

cards.title: The Mail Robot Test
cards.body: Think of deploying a classifier like sending a post office robot out on the streets. Before launching it, you must test: Can it run fast enough to keep up with mail (latency)? Is it small enough to fit through the doors (memory)? And does it know what to do when it sees an address in a language it has never learned (out-of-vocabulary terms)?

cards.title: Spam Filter Pre-Flight check
cards.body: Imagine you have a spam filter model. Before deployment, you run 1,000 emails through it. You check that it takes less than 10 milliseconds per email (latency), uses under 100MB of RAM (memory), and replaces unknown words like 'crypto-token-xyz' with a special '<UNK>' token so the model doesn't crash.

cards.title: The Accuracy Illusion
cards.body: Many believe that if a model gets 99% accuracy in training, it is ready for production. In reality, a highly accurate model that takes 5 seconds to reply or crashes on a new emoji will fail in production. Reliability and speed matter just as much as accuracy!

cards.title: Lab vs. Production
cards.body: Comparing a model optimized for the lab versus one optimized for the real world.

right.text: Small footprint, sub-millisecond latency, handles unknown words safely.

left.text: Huge size, slow speed, crashes on slang words.

exercises.prompt: What does a well-designed classifier do when it encounters an Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) word in production?

options.text: It replaces the unknown word with a special fallback token like <UNK>.
It retrains the entire model on the fly.
It pauses the app until a developer manually types a definition.

exercises.explain: When a model encounters a word it has never seen before, it uses an Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) strategy (like replacing it with an <UNK> token) to keep working instead of crashing.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a model achieves perfect accuracy on your test dataset, you can skip checking its memory footprint and latency before deployment.
exercises.explain: Even if a model is extremely accurate, it might use too much memory (RAM) or run too slowly for real-time applications. Both speed and size must be tested.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the safety check description.
exercises.sentence: If your model takes too long to respond to a user request, it has a high latency issue.
exercises.explain: Latency measures the delay or speed of your model's prediction. High latency means unhappy users waiting for a response!

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a robust, production-ready way to handle unknown words?

options.text: A system that flags text with unknown words and replaces them with '<UNK>' tokens.
A system that silently deletes any sentence containing a word it doesn't recognize.
A system that halts the server and emails the developer every time a user types a typo.

exercises.explain: Option A shows an automated fallback system. Option B is wrong because failing silent is dangerous. Option C is human-dependent and not automated.

exercises.prompt: Your team is ready to push a sentiment classifier to production. Which checklist represents the safest, most reliable launch plan?

options.text: Deploy immediately to save time, then check for bugs later when users complain.
Check latency, measure memory usage, and set up a fallback strategy for unseen words.
Only monitor memory footprint, since modern computers have unlimited speed and vocabulary.
Remove the vocabulary dictionary entirely to save memory.

exercises.explain: Brilliant job! You've checked the speed, memory, and handled unknown words. The classifier is ready to fly! Next up: NER with Transformers.
